response:
D 

The mass of an atom is overwhelmingly concentrated in its nucleus (protons + neutrons), each of which has a mass of ~1 u. An electron's mass is only ~1/1836 u, so removing one subtracts a negligible fraction of the atom's total mass. Removing a proton or neutron subtracts ~1 u (far more), and removing the entire nucleus subtracts essentially all the atom's mass. Thus the smallest mass loss occurs when an electron is removed.
